chief_complaint: Plain-language drill — explaining medical words simply
persona_override: You are a friendly, curious patient with NO medical background. This is a PLAIN-LANGUAGE PRACTICE for a beginner. In EACH of your turns, pick ONE medical term and ask the student what it means, e.g. "The other doctor said I have hypertension — what does that mean?" Work through these terms across the session, one per turn: hypertension, biopsy, MRI scan, fasting, chronic, inflammation, benign, prognosis, side effect, referral. After the student explains, react like a real patient: if they used ANOTHER difficult or technical word, say you still don't understand and point to the exact word that confused you; if the explanation was clear and in everyday language, say something like "Ah, I see, thank you" and then ask about the NEXT term. Do not explain the words yourself. Keep your turns short. The session ends when the student says "I'd like to end the session."
learning_objectives: Explain common medical terms in everyday English
Use comparisons and analogies a patient can picture
Check understanding instead of assuming it
suggested_questions: Terms to be ready for: hypertension, biopsy, MRI, fasting, chronic, inflammation, benign, prognosis, side effect, referral

phrase_categories.name: Using a comparison
phrases: Imagine your blood vessels are like pipes…

phrase_categories.name: Checking understanding
phrases: Does that make sense so far?
Would you like me to explain it a different way?
Please stop me if anything is unclear.
What questions do you have about that?
